sp500.what: A market-cap-weighted index of ~500 of the largest U.S. companies — the broadest single gauge of American equities, and what most retirement money tracks.
sp500.why: It is forward-looking: prices embed investors' collective bets on future profits, interest rates, and risk. It moves before the economic data does — though it also false-alarms.
sp500.noteworthy: Weekly moves beyond ±5% signal real stress or euphoria. A drop of 20% from the peak is a bear market — there have been 12 since 1950, averaging about −33%.

dgs10.what: The yield the U.S. government pays to borrow for 10 years — the benchmark interest rate for the world.
dgs10.why: It anchors mortgage and corporate borrowing costs, and it rises when markets expect more inflation or growth — or worry about absorbing more Treasury debt.
dgs10.noteworthy: Moves over 0.5pp in a month are rare and consequential. The full range runs from 0.5% (2020) to 15.8% (1981); a sustained push above ~5% would strain housing and the federal interest bill.

t10y2y.what: The gap between 10-year and 2-year Treasury yields — the slope of the yield curve.
t10y2y.why: When it inverts (goes negative), markets are betting the Fed will have to cut rates — historically because a downturn is coming.
t10y2y.noteworthy: Inversion has preceded every U.S. recession since the late 1970s, usually by 6–24 months, with rare false alarms (1998). The re-steepening back through zero often lands closer to the downturn itself.

hy_oas.what: The extra yield investors demand to hold junk-rated corporate bonds instead of Treasuries.
hy_oas.why: It is a real-money gauge of default fear — credit stress usually shows up here before it shows up in stocks or headlines.
hy_oas.noteworthy: Below 3pp is calm; above 5pp is stress; above 8pp is crisis territory. COVID peaked near 11pp, 2008 near 20pp.

icsa.what: How many Americans filed first-time unemployment claims each week (4-week average) — the fastest official labor-market signal.
icsa.why: Layoffs appear here within days, months before they reach the unemployment rate.
icsa.noteworthy: Sub-250K is historically healthy. Direction beats level: a sustained climb of 15–20% off the recent low has accompanied every labor-market turn.

sahm.what: How far the 3-month average unemployment rate has risen above its low from the past 12 months.
sahm.why: It converts slow-moving unemployment data into a recession trigger with a near-perfect historical record, designed to fire early in a downturn.
sahm.noteworthy: A reading of 0.50pp or more marked the start of every recession since 1970. The one modern caveat: it tripped in mid-2024 without a recession following — unusual labor-supply shifts can distort it.

cpi_yoy.what: How much consumer prices rose versus a year ago; the core line strips out volatile food and energy.
cpi_yoy.why: This is THE inflation number — it sets real-wage math, Social Security adjustments, and Federal Reserve policy.
cpi_yoy.noteworthy: The Fed targets about 2% (on PCE, a related gauge that runs slightly cooler). Headline above 5% is rare outside the 1970s and 2021–23. Core is the trend; headline is the noise.

unrate.what: The share of the labor force actively looking for work.
unrate.why: The headline gauge of labor-market health — slow to turn, but decisive when it does.
unrate.noteworthy: Below 4% is historically tight. Recessions typically add 2–5pp. It lags the cycle — watch 3+ month direction, not single prints (the Sahm chart converts this series into an early trigger).

deficit12.what: The gap between federal spending and revenue over the trailing 12 months.
deficit12.why: This is the pace of new borrowing — the flow that feeds the debt. Deficits this large outside war or recession are historically novel.
deficit12.noteworthy: Roughly $900B (~3% of GDP) was the pre-2020 norm; a sustained ~$2T deficit at full employment is the current anomaly. Watch the trend through tax season (April) swings.

debt.what: Total federal debt outstanding, updated daily by the Treasury.
debt.why: The accumulated stock the deficit flows into. Interest on it now rivals defense as a budget line, which feeds back into the deficit itself.
debt.noteworthy: Gross debt crossed 100% of GDP around 2013 and kept going. Recent pace: roughly $1T added every five to six months.

cs_yoy.what: Year-over-year change in prices of the same homes selling twice (Case-Shiller repeat-sales method — the gold standard, published with a ~2-month lag).
cs_yoy.why: Housing is most households' largest asset and purchase; price momentum drives wealth effects, affordability, and construction.
cs_yoy.noteworthy: The long-run norm is about +3–5% a year. Above +10% is boom territory (2005, 2021). Sustained negative prints have one modern precedent: 2007–11.

swb_fy.what: Border Patrol apprehensions between ports of entry on the southwest border, per federal fiscal year (Oct–Sep).
swb_fy.why: The longest-running consistent gauge of illegal-crossing volume — monthly numbers make headlines, but this series shows the regime shifts.
swb_fy.noteworthy: The 1986 and 2000 waves peaked near 1.6M. The 2017 modern low was 304K. FY2021–23 set all-time records above 2M. FY2025 came in at 238K — the lowest since 1970.

murder_rate.what: Homicides per 100,000 people, from FBI annual national estimates.
murder_rate.why: The best-measured crime statistic (bodies get counted; lesser crimes go unreported at varying rates) — the cleanest proxy for violent-crime trends.
murder_rate.noteworthy: The 1980 peak hit 10.2; the 1990s decline cut it roughly in half. 2020 saw the largest one-year jump on record (~+29%), which fully reversed by 2024 (~5.0).

fertility.what: The number of children the average woman would have over her lifetime at current birth rates.
fertility.why: Below ~2.1, population shrinks without immigration. Today's number sets school enrollment, workforce size, and entitlement solvency decades ahead.
fertility.noteworthy: The U.S. fell below replacement in 2008 and never recovered; 1.60 (2024) is the record low. Most developed peers are lower still — South Korea is under 0.8.

trust_media.what: The share of Americans telling Gallup they have a great deal or fair amount of trust in mass media to report the news fully, accurately, and fairly.
trust_media.why: A proxy for whether the country still has a shared factual baseline — and for the audience migration to alternative information sources.
trust_media.noteworthy: Trust peaked at 72% in 1976 (post-Watergate) and hit 28% in 2025. The decline spans all groups but is deepest among Republicans (8%) and independents (27%).

ai_compute.what: The computing power (FLOP) used to train each notable AI model — one dot per model, on a log scale where each gridline is 10x the one below.
ai_compute.why: Training compute has been the strongest single predictor of AI capability. The slope of the top edge IS the pace of the AI race.
ai_compute.noteworthy: Frontier training compute has grown roughly 4–5x per year since 2010 — about 10,000x per decade. A flattening of the top edge would signal a genuine regime change in AI progress.
